skillName: Money problems involving addition and subtraction
misconceptionSummary: Students mix pounds and pence, treating £3.50 and 50p as the same unit.

lines.text: Convert everything to the same unit first
Never mix £ and p in the same calculation

steps.narration: Before calculating, convert all amounts into the same unit — either all in pounds as decimals or all in pence as integers. Mixing the two gives nonsense answers.
Money addition is decimal addition — pounds are ones and pence are hundredths. Align the decimal points and add. The answer is seven pounds fifty-two.
Change problems are subtractions. Pad ten pounds as ten point zero zero, subtract six point three seven, and the change is three pounds sixty-three.

visuals.label: Consistent units → align decimal points → subtract

steps.narration: Three pounds sixty-three change. The method is identical to decimal subtraction — money is just a real-world context for the same skill.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: adding £3.50 + 75p as 3.50 + 75 = 78.50. Convert to the same unit first: £3.50 + £0.75 = £4.25, or 350p + 75p = 425p = £4.25.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Pounds and pence must be in the same unit before any calculation. Convert everything to pounds (decimals) or everything to pence (integers) — never mix the two.
